FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline and 1-year follow-up, N=9,066 children aged 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal models with psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time categories one year later

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology, 2024; no indication of preprint or duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Paper explicitly measures and separately analyzes "social screen time" (texting + social media + video chatting) and "nonsocial screen time" (YouTube + videogames). These phone/SM-specific variables (texting, social media, video chat) are paired with individual-trait variables (psychiatric symptoms: internalizing, externalizing, attention, behavioral problems). Methods state: "social and nonsocial screen time variables, which summed time spent texting, using social media, and video chatting separately on weekends and weekdays, and nonsocial screen time variables, which summed time spent watching shows or movies, watching videos on YouTube, and playing video games"; analyses report ORs for psychiatric symptoms predicting weekday/weekend social and nonsocial screen use (e.g., OR=1.22 for clinical problems → weekday nonsocial)

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: regression ORs with 95% CIs reported for all SM/screen time analyses (e.g., OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.22–1.23; OR=1.10, 95% CI 1.09–1.11; OR=1.65, 95% CI 1.63–1.67)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric results: logistic regression models with covariate control (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, baseline screen use and symptoms)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline → 1-year), but SM is not DV-only. Paper includes both directions: psychiatric symptoms → screen time (main) AND screen time → psychiatric symptoms (supplementary analyses); thus SM appears as IV in some analyses, disqualifying EC7
notes: Bidirectional associations explicitly tested and reported. Composite constructed exclusively from phone/SM/digital-socializing items (texting + social media + video chatting = social screen time). Clear separation of social vs. nonsocial screen use throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline and 2-year follow-up, N≈7,107 at baseline, 4,555 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; causal inference study using Mendelian randomization (MR), linear mixed-effects (LME) models, and mediation analysis with GWAS and ABCD individual-level data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Advanced Science, 2024; no indication of preprint or duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Paper explicitly measures six separate screen use modalities and reports coefficients for each (TV, Video, Game, Text, SNS, VC). Text and SNS are phone/SM-specific variables. Text is texting (phone-based); SNS is social networking sites. Table S1 shows effect estimates (β and p) for each modality separately predicting cognitive (picture vocabulary, pattern comparison, oral reading recognition) and neural outcomes (brain volumes in frontal/temporal regions). Paired with individual-trait cognitive and neural outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: LME model β coefficients and p-values for each screen type (e.g., Text: β=−0.02, p<0.001); Mendelian randomization estimates with 95% CIs (e.g., TV on picture vocabulary β=−2.39, p<0.05); brain volume associations in Table S3 with effect coefficients across 148 cortical and 22 subcortical regions

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis combining observational (LME) and causal inference (Mendelian randomization, two-sample MR) methods with individual-level ABCD data and GWAS summary statistics; numeric results for SM/phone exposure paired with cognitive/neural outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline → 2-year), but SM/screen use is NOT DV-only. Paper explicitly analyzes screen use as IV predicting cognitive and brain development (main causal direction). Cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses both present; screen use acts as predictor, not DV-only
notes: Sophisticated causal analysis (MR) to isolate screen modalities. Text and SNS explicitly analyzed as separate exposures with reported coefficients. Displacement effect (screen use reducing reading time) also tested as mechanism, with reading as mediator on brain development pathway.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, years 2–4 follow-up (baseline year-2 age 12 years), N=6,143 children, 12,745 total observations

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal Bayesian hierarchical linear models examining association between reward processing (MID task fMRI) at year-2 and VGAQ scores over years 2–4

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2024; no indication of preprint or duplicate

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds no phone/SM-specific variable. The paper measures VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire) as the DV. VGAQ is a problematic-use scale measuring video game addiction broadly, not a phone/SM exposure variable. The predictor is reward processing (caudate activation during MID task), which is a neural outcome, not an SM/phone variable. Per binding clarifications (§2.4): 'Gaming alone (VGAQ, IGDS9-SF, console, PC, unspecified-platform, "gaming addiction") fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based OR paired with separately-measured phone/SM variable.' This paper measures generic video gaming (VGAQ) with no platform specification, no separate phone/mobile gaming measurement, and no separate phone/SM variable. Step 2 does not apply (no composite of phone/SM items reported). Paper inverts the required direction: it pairs a neural outcome (IV) with a gaming-addiction scale (DV), not a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait outcome

notes: Paper focuses on neurobiological mechanisms of gaming addiction (reward processing in caudate nucleus). VGAQ not separated by device/platform. No phone/social media variables analyzed. Fails IC2 as a gaming-only paper without phone/SM isolation or pairing.
